source_text: “But why couldn’t the poor woman—” she broke in disconnectedly. Then she left the revolt of her thought unexpressed to cry out: “Oh! It is degrading! It is not nice! It is nasty!”

text: “What is the matter?” said the man who had made Miss Pross scream; and he spoke in English, in a vexed and abrupt voice, though in a low tone.
source_text: “What is the matter?” said the man who had caused Miss Pross to scream; speaking in a vexed, abrupt voice (though in a low tone), and in English.

text: “Ah! mercy, indeed, ma’am,” was the answer. “I do but hope this will teach master never to keep any more of these dreadful creatures, who are born murderers and robbers from their very cradles. Poor Noah! he was all but killed, ma’am, when I came in.”
source_text: “Ah! mercy indeed, ma’am,” was the reply. “I only hope this’ll teach master not to have any more of these dreadful creatures, that are born to be murderers and robbers from their very cradle. Poor Noah! He was all but killed, ma’am, when I came in.”

text: “Well, I was whisperin’ that it’d be a good notion if you could manage to unearth a gentleman friend—for her” (he indicated her companion), “and then we might go off and have an ice-cream soda somewhere, or coffee, or anything of that sort.”

text: Mr. Britling presently resumed the conversation. “For me it came all in a breath, with neither doubt nor hope. I kept hoping to the very end that nothing would come near Hugh. And then it was as though a black shutter had dropped—in a moment....”

text: “There is no harm in it,” said the young man with frankness. “I will make myself known—if you please—Mr. Towers Chandler. After our dinner, which I shall endeavour to render as agreeable as may be, I shall wish you good-evening, or see you safely to your door, as you may choose.”
source_text: “There is nothing wrong about it,” said the young man, candidly. “I’ll introduce myself—permit me—Mr. Towers Chandler. After our dinner, which I will try to make as pleasant as possible, I will bid you good-evening, or attend you safely to your door, whichever you prefer.”

source_text: “Age, about forty years; height, about five feet nine; black hair; complexion dark; generally, rather handsome visage; eyes dark, face thin, long, and sallow; nose aquiline, but not straight, having a peculiar inclination towards the left cheek; expression, therefore, sinister.”

text: “Take a rest, child; at the rate you are consuming the very air of the house, the kingdom will be forced to import it by to-morrow, and the treasury is lean enough already without such an expense.”
source_text: “Take a rest, child; the way you are using up all the domestic air, the kingdom will have to go to importing it by to-morrow, and it's a low enough treasury without that.”

text: “Well, then,” said I, “either I am bereft of my wits, or something no less dreadful has come to pass. Now tell me plainly and truly: where am I?”
source_text: “Well, then,” I said, “either I am a lunatic, or something just as awful has happened. Now tell me, honest and true, where am I?”

text: “What, a map? What do you mean by that? Do you not know what a map is? There, there, never mind, say no more, I dislike explanations; they only cloud a matter till one can make nothing of it. Off with you, dear; good-day; show her the way, Clarence.”
source_text: “What, a map? What are you talking about? Don't you know what a map is? There, there, never mind, don't explain, I hate explanations; they fog a thing up so that you can't tell anything about it. Run along, dear; good-day; show her the way, Clarence.”

text: “Not always those,” said the old gentleman, as he patted Oliver on the head and smiled; “there are others quite as weighty, though of a far lesser size. How should you like to grow up a clever man, and write books, eh?”
source_text: “Not always those,” said the old gentleman, patting Oliver on the head, and smiling as he did so; “there are other equally heavy ones, though of a much smaller size. How should you like to grow up a clever man, and write books, eh?”

text: “No,” said she, “we will have none of this. If she comes in, she must needs see you—and suppose, if she be so inclined, that there is something amiss! But how am I to open the door to her, when she dislikes me—when she desires to see not me, but her son? I will not open the door!”
source_text: “No,” she said, “we won’t have any of this. If she comes in she must see you—and think if she likes there’s something wrong! But how can I open the door to her, when she dislikes me—wishes to see not me, but her son? I won’t open the door!”

text: “None upon earth. I love Elizabeth, and await our union with delight. Let the day, then, be appointed; and on that day I will devote myself, in life or in death, to the happiness of my cousin.”
source_text: “None on earth. I love Elizabeth and look forward to our union with delight. Let the day therefore be fixed; and on it I will consecrate myself, in life or death, to the happiness of my cousin.”

text: “Yes, yes; and I know well enough what that means. It grieves me, Clym. You are spending your life here to no purpose; and all for her sake alone. Had that woman not existed, you would never have thought of this scheme of teaching at all.”
source_text: “Yes, yes; and I know what that amounts to. It troubles me, Clym. You are wasting your life here; and it is solely on account of her. If it had not been for that woman you would never have entertained this teaching scheme at all.”

text: “Well, I heard that you had once been very fond of Eustacia—no more than that, though it was let fall by degrees. You ought not to be angry!”
source_text: “Well, I heard that you used to be very fond of Eustacia—nothing more than that, though dropped in a bit-by-bit way. You ought not to be angry!”

text:
Year that quiver’d and reel’d beneath me!
Your summer wind was warm enow, yet the air I drew did freeze me,
A heavy gloom fell through the sunshine and made darkness of me,
Must I change my conquering songs? said I within myself,
Must I indeed learn to intone the chill dirges of the baffled?
And moody hymns of overthrow?
source_text:
Year that trembled and reel’d beneath me!
Your summer wind was warm enough, yet the air I breathed froze me,
A thick gloom fell through the sunshine and darken’d me,
Must I change my triumphant songs? said I to myself,
Must I indeed learn to chant the cold dirges of the baffled?
And sullen hymns of defeat?

text:
Snuffing near his prey;
Yet he soothes their fears away
When he licks their hands,
And mute beside them stands.
source_text:
Smelling to his prey;
But their fears allay
When he licks their hands,
And silent by them stands.

text:
While I was fearing it, it came,
Yet came with less of fear,
Because that fearing it so long
Had almost made it dear.
There is a fitting to dismay,
A fitting to despair.
'Tis harder knowing it is due,
Than knowing it is there.
The trying on of uttermost,
The morning it is new,
Is more terrible than wearing it
A whole existence through.
source_text:

text:
Some one casts a mattress out, --
The children hasten by;
They wonder if It perished there, --
I used to, when a boy.
source_text:

text:

text:
I shall fashion the true poem of riches,  
To win for the body and the soul whate’er abides and passes onward  
and is not dropt by death;  
I shall pour forth egotism and reveal it under all, and I shall be the  
bard of personality,  
And I shall show of male and female that either is but the equal of  
the other,  
And sexual organs and acts! do ye centre in me, for I am resolv’d  
to tell you with brave, lucid voice, to prove you illustrious,  
And I shall show that there is no flaw in the present, and  
can be none in the future,  
And I shall show that whatsoever befalls any one may be turn’d to  
beauteous ends,  
And I shall show that naught can happen more beauteous than death,  
And I shall weave a thread through my poems that time and events are  
compact,  
And that all the things of the universe are perfect miracles, each  
as deep as any.
source_text:
I will make the true poem of riches,
To earn for the body and the mind whatever adheres and goes forward
and is not dropt by death;
I will effuse egotism and show it underlying all, and I will be the
bard of personality,
And I will show of male and female that either is but the equal of
the other,
And sexual organs and acts! do you concentrate in me, for I am determin’d
to tell you with courageous clear voice to prove you illustrious,
And I will show that there is no imperfection in the present, and
can be none in the future,
And I will show that whatever happens to anybody it may be turn’d to
beautiful results,
And I will show that nothing can happen more beautiful than death,
And I will thread a thread through my poems that time and events are
compact,
And that all the things of the universe are perfect miracles, each
as profound as any.

text:

Thou art a merry, bonnie lass,
But thou hast a wakeful minnie.
source_text:

text:

And leaves the ravelings behind;
O housewife of the evening west,
Come back, and dust the pond!

text:
I mused upon the griefs of ruined man,
I mused upon the unrelenting wrath of Kings;
My enrapt soul abode on those that bind
The tangled volume of intermingling things,
When fell and wild disorder unto man grim sorrow brings.
source_text:
I pondered on the woes of lost mankind,
I pondered on the ceaseless rage of Kings;
My rapt soul dwelt upon the ties that bind
The mazy volume of commingling things,
When fell and wild misrule to man stern sorrow brings.

text:

When no one is here, --
Will the gown wherein I wept
Answer me to wear?
source_text:
That which sings so, speaks so,

text:
The bee is not afeared of me,  
I know the butterfly;  
The comely people of the wood  
Receive me cordially.
source_text:
The bee is not afraid of me,
I know the butterfly;
The pretty people in the woods
Receive me cordially.

text:

I here embody and invoke my themes, that they may pass before ye.
source_text:
And by the spells which ye vouchsafe to those your ministers in earnest,

text:
Of bronze and flame
The north, this night!
How meet its shapes,
How concerted with itself,
How far from any alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
Toward universe, or me,
It stains my simple spirit
With hues of majesty,
Till I assume more ample postures,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance in them.
source_text:
Of bronze and blaze
The north, to-night!
So adequate its forms,
So preconcerted with itself,
So distant to alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
To universe, or me,
It paints my simple spirit
With tints of majesty,
Till I take vaster attitudes,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance of them.

text:
You left for me the bounds of pain,
Vast as the heaving sea,
Set betwixt eternity and time,
Your mind, and mine, and me.
source_text:
You left me boundaries of pain
Capacious as the sea,
Between eternity and time,
Your consciousness and me.

text:
The lover may shimmer and shine,  
Drawing near to his bonnie, bright plaything:  
Yet wedlock will quickly make plain  
He has won but a drest-up naething.
source_text:
The lover may sparkle and glow,
Approaching his bonie bit gay thing:
But marriage will soon let him know
He’s gotten—a buskit up naething.

text:
Poor Man, the fly, doth oft hum past thy side,  
And oft, by chance, as he draweth thee nigh,  
Thy cursed old elbow itcheth up for joy  
And hellish pleasure!  
Already in thy fancy’s eye,  
Thy sicker treasure.
source_text:
Poor Man, the flie, aft bizzes by,
And aft, as chance he comes thee nigh,
Thy damn’d auld elbow yeuks wi’joy
And hellish pleasure!
Already in thy fancy’s eye,
Thy sicker treasure.

text:

His head, a cap and frill.  
He laboreth at every tree, --  
A worm his dearest goal.
source_text:

text:
PANTHEA:
Only a feeling
Abides of them, as doth the sovereign healing
Of music, when the rapt voice and the lute
Grow faint, before the answering tones are mute,
Which through the dark and labyrinthine soul,
Like echoes through vast caverns, wind and roll.
source_text:
PANTHEA:
Only a sense
Remains of them, like the omnipotence
Of music, when the inspired voice and lute
Languish, ere yet the responses are mute,
Which through the deep and labyrinthine soul,
Like echoes through long caverns, wind and roll.

text:
Throb! throb, ye drums!--blare! bugles, blare!
Above the traffic of cities--above the clamor of wheels in the streets;
Are couches prepared for sleepers by night in the houses? no sleepers
may lie in those couches,
No trader’s bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--should
they endure?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer essay to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to plead his cause before the judge?
Then clatter swifter, weightier drums--ye bugles, wilder, blow.
source_text:
Beat! beat! drums!--blow! bugles! blow!
Over the traffic of cities--over the rumble of wheels in the streets;
Are beds prepared for sleepers at night in the houses? no sleepers
must sleep in those beds,
No bargainers’ bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--would
they continue?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer attempt to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to state his case before the judge?
Then rattle quicker, heavier drums--you bugles wilder blow.

text:
Great Father, thou dost clearly know before  
That all I utter unto thee is sooth;  
I am a man most veracious evermore,  
And wholly unacquainted with untruth.  
At sunrise Phoebus came, but with no band  
Of Gods to bear him witness, in great wrath,  
Unto my dwelling, seeking there his heifers,  
And saying that I needs must show him where they are,
source_text:

text:
It is not to dissolve thee that thou wert born of thy mother and
father, it is to know thee,
It is not that thou shouldst be wavering, but that thou shouldst be resolute,
Something long in making and shapeless is come and made in thee,
Thou art from hence secure, whatever cometh or departeth.
source_text:
It is not to diffuse you that you were born of your mother and
father, it is to identify you,
It is not that you should be undecided, but that you should be decided,
Something long preparing and formless is arrived and form’d in you,
You are henceforth secure, whatever comes or goes.

text:
A carol ending sixty-nine—a close—a reiteration,  
My lines in joy and hope still running on the selfsame line,  
Of thee, O God, Life, Nature, Freedom, Poetry;  
Of thee, my Land—thy rivers, prairies, States—thou dappled Flag I love,  
Thy total kept entire—of north, south, east, and west, thy  
all the items;  
Of me myself—the jocund heart yet beating in my breast,  
The body wreck’d, old, poor, and paralyzed—the strange inertia  
falling like a pall around me,  
The burning fires beneath my sluggish blood not yet extinct,  
The undiminish’d faith—the clustered groups of loving friends.
source_text:
A carol closing sixty-nine--a resume--a repetition,
My lines in joy and hope continuing on the same,
Of ye, O God, Life, Nature, Freedom, Poetry;
Of you, my Land--your rivers, prairies, States--you, mottled Flag I love,
Your aggregate retain’d entire--Of north, south, east and west, your
items all;
Of me myself--the jocund heart yet beating in my breast,
The body wreck’d, old, poor and paralyzed--the strange inertia
falling pall-like round me,
The burning fires down in my sluggish blood not yet extinct,
The undiminish’d faith--the groups of loving friends.

text:
Attend close, my dear scholars,
Doctrines, politics, and civilization spring from you,
Sculpture and monuments and any thing inscribed anywhere are reckoned in you,
The essence of histories and statistics, as far back as the records
reach, is in you this hour, and myths and tales the same,
If you were not breathing and walking here, where would they all be?
The most renown’d poems would be ashes, orations and plays would
be vacuities.
source_text:
List close my scholars dear,
Doctrines, politics and civilization exurge from you,
Sculpture and monuments and any thing inscribed anywhere are tallied in you,
The gist of histories and statistics as far back as the records
reach is in you this hour, and myths and tales the same,
If you were not breathing and walking here, where would they all be?
The most renown’d poems would be ashes, orations and plays would
be vacuums.

text: “Ah, fair my lord, pray thou him! Thou canst, if thou wilt. Ah, he suffereth so; and it is for me—for _me_! And how can I endure it? Would I might behold him die—a sweet, swift death; oh, my Hugo, I cannot bear this one!”

text:
“True, true,” said Joseph Poorgrass. “The Balls were ever a most excitable kind.  
I knew the boy’s grandsire—a man most nervously modest, aye, even to genteel refin’dness.  
It was blush, blush with him, almost as much as ’tis with me—  
not but what that, in me, ’tis a fault!”
source_text: “True, true,” said Joseph Poorgrass. “The Balls were always a very excitable family. I knowed the boy’s grandfather—a truly nervous and modest man, even to genteel refinery. ’Twas blush, blush with him, almost as much as ’tis with me—not but that ’tis a fault in me!”

text:

Her, armed with sorrow sore;  
Till before their path  
A couching lion lay.
source_text:

Till before their way
A couching lion lay.

text:
Open throat of my soul, pouring forth gladness,  
Eyes of my soul beholding perfection,  
Natural life within me, loyal in praise of things,  
Evermore attesting the triumph of things.
source_text:
Open mouth of my soul uttering gladness,
Eyes of my soul seeing perfection,
Natural life of me faithfully praising things,
Corroborating forever the triumph of things.

text:
They say that Time assuageth, --
Time never did assuage;
A very sorrow waxes strong,
As sinews do, with age.
source_text:
They say that 'time assuages,' --
Time never did assuage;
An actual suffering strengthens,
As sinews do, with age.

text:
But wi’ a Lord!—my shin doth bear,
A Lord—a Peer—an Earl’s own heir!
Yet higher still, my bonnet!
And sic a Lord!—lang Scots ells twa,
Our Peerage he o’erlooks them a’,
As I o’erlook my sonnet.
source_text:
But wi’ a Lord!—stand out my shin,
A Lord—a Peer—an Earl’s son!
Up higher yet, my bonnet
An’ sic a Lord!—lang Scoth ells twa,
Our Peerage he o’erlooks them a’,
As I look o’er my sonnet.

text:
ULYSSES:
Attend, then, what chastisement I devise
For this fell monster, and what sure escape
From your hard servitude.
source_text:
ULYSSES:
Listen then what a punishment I have
For this fell monster, how secure a flight
From your hard servitude.

text:
CHORUS OF SPIRITS AND HOURS:  
Then twist the web of the mystic measure;  
From the deeps of the sky and the borders of earth,  
Come, fleet Spirits of power and of pleasure,  
Swell the dance and the music of mirth,  
As the waves of a thousand streams sweep by  
To an ocean of splendour and harmony!
source_text:
CHORUS OF SPIRITS AND HOURS:
Then weave the web of the mystic measure;
From the depths of the sky and the ends of the earth,
Come, swift Spirits of might and of pleasure,
Fill the dance and the music of mirth,
As the waves of a thousand streams rush by
To an ocean of splendour and harmony!

text:
More popular poets robe the ideal in
familiar and sensible
imagery. Shelley loved to idealize the real—to endow the mechanism of
the material universe with a soul and a voice, and to bestow these also
upon the most delicate and abstract emotions and thoughts of the mind.
Sophocles was his great master in this kind of imagery.
source_text:
More popular poets clothe the ideal with familiar and sensible
imagery. Shelley loved to idealize the real—to gift the mechanism of
the material universe with a soul and a voice, and to bestow such also
on the most delicate and abstract emotions and thoughts of the mind.
Sophocles was his great master in this species of imagery.

text:
Dear friend, whoever thou art, receive this kiss,  
I bestow it especially on thee, forget me not,  
I feel as one who has finished the day’s labor and may retire awhile,  
I take once more my many translations, from my avataras  
ascending, while others doubtless still await me,  
An unknown sphere, more real than I had dream’d, more near, darts  
awakening rays about me, So long!  
Bear in mind my words, I may return again,  
I love thee, I depart from matter,  
I am as one unbodied, triumphant, dead.
source_text:
Dear friend whoever you are take this kiss,
I give it especially to you, do not forget me,
I feel like one who has done work for the day to retire awhile,
I receive now again of my many translations, from my avataras
ascending, while others doubtless await me,
An unknown sphere more real than I dream’d, more direct, darts
awakening rays about me, So long!
Remember my words, I may again return,
I love you, I depart from materials,
I am as one disembodied, triumphant, dead.

text:
Her chilling finger on my brow,
With frostiest touch she bound my soul—
Cold as the finger of the dead,
Or dews that round a tombstone roll—
source_text:
Her chilling finger on my head,
With coldest touch congealed my soul—
Cold as the finger of the dead,
Or damps which round a tombstone roll—

text:
O, what is death but parting breath?
On many a sanguine plain
I’ve faced his look, and in this hour
I scorn him once again!
Sae rantingly, &c.
source_text:
O, what is death but parting breath?
On many a bloody plain
I’ve dared his face, and in this place
I scorn him yet again!
Sae rantingly, &c.

text:
LUCRETIA [IN A HURRIED AND CONFUSED MANNER]:
I think he sleeps;
Yet wake him not, I pray thee, grant me time awhile,
He is a wicked and a wrathful man;
Should he be roused from out his sleep to-night,
Which is, I know, a hell of angry dreams,
It were not good; indeed it were not good.
Wait till day break...
[ASIDE.]
Oh, I am deadly sick!
source_text:
LUCRETIA [IN A HURRIED AND CONFUSED MANNER]:
I think he sleeps;
Yet, wake him not, I pray, spare me awhile,
He is a wicked and a wrathful man;
Should he be roused out of his sleep to-night,
Which is, I know, a hell of angry dreams,
It were not well; indeed it were not well.
Wait till day break...
[ASIDE.]
Oh, I am deadly sick!

text:
As in a vision waking,
E’en while I chant, I watch it rise, and mark and foretell, without and within,
Its manifold array.
source_text:
As in a waking vision,
E’en while I chant I see it rise, I scan and prophesy outside and in,
Its manifold ensemble.

text:
An’ noo we’re pent in dens an’ hollows,
And chased, as was brave William Wallace,
Wi’ constables—thae blackguard fallows,
An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!
source_text:
An’ now we’re dern’d in dens and hollows,
And hunted, as was William Wallace,
Wi’ constables-thae blackguard fallows,
An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!

text:
From thee, Eliza, must I go,
And from my native shore;
The cruel fates between us throw
A boundless ocean’s roar:
Yet boundless oceans, roaring wide,
Between my love and me,
Can never, never divide
My heart and soul from thee.
source_text:
From thee, Eliza, I must go,
And from my native shore;
The cruel fates between us throw
A boundless ocean’s roar:
But boundless oceans, roaring wide,
Between my love and me,
They never, never can divide
My heart and soul from thee.

text:
For a’ his meal and a’ his maut,
For a’ his fresh beef and his saut,
For a’ his gowd and white sillerie,
And auld men shall never daunt me.
To daunt me, &c.
source_text:
For a’ his meal and a’ his maut,
For a’ his fresh beef and his saut,
For a’ his gold and white monie,
And auld men shall never daunton me.
To daunton me, &c.

text:
Now life’s cold evening lays dim shadows on thine eye,  
And ushers the long, dreary night:  
Yet thou, like the star that athwart gilds the sky,  
Dost hold thy course to the latest in light.
source_text:
Now life’s chilly evening dim shades on your eye,
And ushers the long dreary night:
But you, like the star that athwart gilds the sky,
Your course to the latest is bright.

text:
Eagle! why dost thou mount above that tomb?
To what high and star-pavèd heaven-home
Dost thou sail?—
I am the likeness of fleet Plato’s soul,
Ascending unto heaven; Athens holds
His body here below.
source_text:
Eagle! why soarest thou above that tomb?
To what sublime and star-ypaven home
Floatest thou?—
I am the image of swift Plato’s spirit,
Ascending heaven; Athens doth inherit
His corpse below.

text:
“I may not stay a minute, thank ye,”  
he answered to her greeting.  
“I am driving to East Egdon;  
but I came round here but to tell you the news.  
Perhaps you have heard—  
of Mr. Wildeve’s fortune?”
source_text: “I can’t stay a minute, thank ye,” he answered to her greeting. “I am driving to East Egdon; but I came round here just to tell you the news. Perhaps you have heard—about Mr. Wildeve’s fortune?”

text:
A day that ne’er shall fade away,
So far I scaled the upland way,
I din’d with one of noble rank.
source_text:
A ne’er-to-be-forgotten day,
Sae far I sprackl’d up the brae,
I dinner’d wi’ a Lord.

text:
Her small form at the gate  
The angels sure descried,  
For I could never seek her  
On the frail, mortal side.
source_text:
Her little figure at the gate
The angels must have spied,
Since I could never find her
Upon the mortal side.

text:
Spirit of Nature! here  
In this unending wilderness  
Of worlds, beneath whose folded vastness  
Even high-wing'd Fancy reels,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.  
Yet not the lightest leaf  
That trembles at the passing gale  
Is less alive with thee,—  
Yet not the humblest worm  
That hides in graves and feeds upon the dead  
Less partakes thy eternal breath.  
Spirit of Nature! thou  
Imperishable as this glorious scene,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.
source_text:
Spirit of Nature! here
In this interminable wilderness
Of worlds, at whose involved immensity
Even soaring fancy staggers,
Here is thy fitting temple.
Yet not the lightest leaf
That quivers to the passing breeze
Is less instinct with thee,—
Yet not the meanest worm.
That lurks in graves and fattens on the dead,
Less shares thy eternal breath.
Spirit of Nature! thou
Imperishable as this glorious scene,
Here is thy fitting temple.
